mappings.rationaleAI: The Vedas as the oldest scriptures of Hinduism composed in Vedic Sanskrit from the 2nd millennium BCE directly address the Vedic period (A.3) and primary scriptures regarded as eternal revelations (B.1.a). The Rigveda, Yajurveda, Samaveda, and Atharvaveda with their Samhitas, Brahmanas, Aranyakas, and Upanishads constitute Hindu sacred literature (B.1).

Jainism with its 24 Tirthankaras, radical non-violence (ahimsa), Mahavira's teachings, Digambara and Shvetambara sects, and karmic philosophy directly addresses Jainism as another religion of India (D.1), including its history, beliefs, practices, and institutions.

Shankara's systematization of Advaita Vedanta in the 8th century connects to the rise of devotional Hinduism (A.7). His commentaries on the Upanishads and Brahma Sutras represent Hindu philosophy (B.3). His assertion of the identity of Atman with Brahman addresses views about God or the sacred (C.1.a).
Buddhism's origins in the eastern Gangetic Plain in the 5th century BCE as a challenge to Brahmanism directly address the heterodox period of challenges by reformers and ascetic groups (A.4).

Sikhism founded by Guru Nanak in the Punjab, developed through ten Gurus, with the Guru Granth Sahib, Khalsa, and the five Ks directly addresses Sikhism as another religion of India (D.2), covering its history, literature, beliefs, practices, and institutions.

The Gita as post-Vedic Sanskrit literature constitutes sacred literature (B.1.b). Its synthesis of dharma, karma yoga, jnana yoga, and bhakti yoga addresses Hindu philosophy (B.3) and the three margas (C.1.c.iii). Vedantic non-dualism and Samkhya cosmology address views about God (C.1.a). Karma and samsara doctrines connect to doctrines of karman and transmigration (C.1.c.ii). Its Vaishnavism connects to that form of Hinduism (C.2.b).

The Indus Valley Civilisation directly relates to the prehistoric and protohistoric periods, specifically the religions of the indigenous prehistoric peoples and of the Indus Valley civilization (A.2).

The Gupta Empire's Hindu religious consolidation including Mahabharata and Ramayana canonization connects to the Puranic period (A.6). Gupta-era patronage of arts and literature relates to cultural expressions of Hindu values (C.4).
Gandhi's Hindu ethics, nonviolent resistance, and role in Indian independence connect to the modern period of Hinduism (A.9) and ethical, social, and nationalist movements in modern Hinduism (C.2.f).
Akbar's religious pluralism and Din-i Ilahi connecting Islam, Hinduism, and Zoroastrianism relate to Hinduism during the age of bhakti (A.8) and the Islamic and Hindu background of Sikhism (D.2.a.i).
The Mughal Empire's Hindu-Muslim relations and multicultural governance provide the historical context for the age of bhakti (A.8) and the history of Sikhism during the Mughal period (D.2.a).
Siddhartha Gautama as a wandering ascetic who challenged Brahmanical orthodoxy directly relates to the heterodox period of challenges to Brahmanism by reformers and ascetic groups (A.4).
Vedic recitation as a major oral tradition connects to the transmission of primary scriptures through elaborate mnemonic systems (B.1.a).
The cross-cultural typology of divine beings including Hindu conceptualizations addresses views about God or the sacred (C.1.a), and connects to Vaisnavism (C.2.b) and Saivism (C.2.c).
The Achaemenid Empire's Zoroastrian religion connects to Parsiism as Zoroastrianism in India (D.3).
The history of art covering Indian traditions connects to cultural expressions of Hindu values through art, symbols, and images (C.4).
The history of Indian philosophy including Vedanta, Samkhya, Nyaya, and Mimamsa directly relates to Hindu philosophy (B.3).
Religion as a comprehensive concept broadly connects to the history of Hinduism (A), beliefs and practices of Hinduism (C), and other religions of India (D).
British colonial rule in India provides the context for the modern period of Hinduism (A.9) and the condition of Sikhism under British rule (D.2.a.v).
European colonialism in India provides the context for the modern period of Hinduism (A.9) and its reformist responses.
Alexander's conquests reaching northwestern India and the rise of Greco-Buddhism relate to the heterodox period's cross-cultural interactions (A.4).
